## What you're seeing

Those aren't stuck torrents. Take your first example, `246C4643…9398.meta`:

- **Complete**: seeding, 100%, 137 MB
- **Saved correctly**: `/downloads/TV Shows/The Bad Batch (2021)`
- **File on disk has the right name**: `Star.Wars.the.Bad.Batch.S03E02.1080p.HEVC.x265-MeGusta[EZTVx.to].mkv`

Only the *torrent's display name* is wrong. The content downloaded, got named, and filed correctly.

## Why the name is a hash

The name is a **display-name override stored inside qBittorrent**. Its `.fastresume` file literally contains:

```
qBt-name: 246C46439929E36F77561F3C3C62D5839ACFCAB6.meta
```

…while the same torrent's real metadata is correct (`info.name` = the Star Wars filename). qBittorrent pins that override at add time and never revisits it, so it survives metadata resolution and completion forever.

Where did that string come from? It's rTorrent's naming convention — uppercase infohash + `.meta` — and that led me to the actual root cause:

**rTorrent's session directory is misconfigured to point at your downloads directory.** It's been scattering its magnet-metadata placeholder files *into your media folders*:

```
/downloads/TV Shows/Will Trent (2023)/00E6695AAD7D2DA2D2C6F769C057B2EEE57FF1A6.meta
/downloads/TV Shows/The Bad Batch (2021)/246C46439929E36F77561F3C3C62D5839ACFCAB6.meta
```

There are **444 of these junk files sitting in your library folders** right now. When those torrents were moved onto qBittorrent, the `.meta` filenames came along as the display names.

## Scale

| | synoplex | ehr-qnap |
|---|---|---|
| Torrents named `*.meta` | **268 live in qBittorrent** (17 at 100%) | 29 |
| Stray `.meta` files in media folders | **444** | — |

rTorrent itself is now empty (0 torrents), so everything lives in qBittorrent.

## One thing to know about UltraTorrent's part in this

The app shows whatever the engine reports, refreshed every 2 seconds — so it isn't caching a stale name. But it also has **no repair path**: no logic to notice a hash-shaped name and fall back to the real one, and the rTorrent `renameTorrent` implementation is a silent no-op (it writes `d.custom.set`, which nothing ever reads back). There's also no rename route or UI, so you currently *can't* fix a bad name by hand.

3. **Delete the 444 stray `.meta` files** from the library folders.
4. **Ship a code fix**: fall back to the real name when the engine reports a hash-shaped one, and expose a working rename.

## What I did

**1. Renamed the broken torrents** — 78 on synoplex, using qBittorrent's own file list to recover each real name. `246C4643….meta` → `Star.Wars.the.Bad.Batch.S03E02.1080p.HEVC.x265-MeGusta[EZTVx.to].mkv`, and so on. Your qnap had none live. The remaining 244 are magnets that never fetched metadata — there's literally no name to recover, and your parking queue already has them flagged as dead swarms.

**2. Corrected my own diagnosis.** I told you the rTorrent session path was misconfigured. **It wasn't** — it's already `/downloads/.session`. The `.meta` files are rTorrent's magnet *placeholder content*, which it writes into each torrent's destination folder by design, and which got orphaned when the metadata never resolved. I also checked qBittorrent's DHT/PEX/LSD: all on, so there's no config to fix there either. Good thing to know rather than have me "fix" a healthy config.

**3. Cleared the library** — 450 orphaned `.meta` files removed from your media folders (444 synoplex, 6 qnap), backed up first to `/downloads/.rtorrent-meta-orphans-backup.tgz` on each host. Both now report **0 remaining**. I only matched strictly hash-named files, and verified none of your `.meta` files were legitimate.

**4. Shipped the code fix** (`b2027e6`) so this self-heals instead of needing me:

- `TorrentNameRepairService` detects placeholder names and repairs them **at the engine**, so the list, search, parking queue and automation log all see the real name. It won't guess when paths disagree, won't touch a magnet that's still resolving, and won't retry a refusing engine every 2 seconds.
- **`rTorrent.renameTorrent` was lying** — it wrote `d.custom.set(hash,'name',…)`, a custom key nothing ever reads back, so it returned success and changed nothing. It now throws.
- **`torrent_snapshots` was leaking** — it only ever upserted, so every torrent ever removed stayed forever (**2,316 rows against 562 live torrents**). That's a real correctness bug beyond cosmetics: search and acquisition's "do we already have this?" dedupe were matching torrents that no longer exist. Now pruned each sync.

Backend builds clean; 840 tests pass plus 13 new ones. Three failures exist (`SystemService.version`, `organizeLibrary`) but I verified they fail identically on clean `HEAD` — pre-existing, not mine.

- Settings table: `missingSearchProfileId` described as "Quality profile for grabs." It's dead — read from settings then passed as `_settingsProfileId` and never used (`missing-episode-search.service.ts:139-143`).

**Claude:** The API/security agent's findings are worth surfacing now, because two are serious and one is security-relevant:

**`SECURITY.md` was silent about API keys** while the table, DTOs, and `apikeys.manage` permission all exist — which invites a reader to assume the feature works. It now says plainly that **an API key cannot authenticate a request**: `keyHash` is written and *read nowhere*, and `JwtStrategy` is the only strategy. So `scopes` are never enforced and a revoked or expired key isn't rejected — because nothing consults keys at all. The minting side is sound (Argon2id, shown once), so it's inert rather than exploitable, but a security control that only *looks* real is worse than an absent one.

**`SECURITY.md` also had the `POWER_USER` role wrong** — it claimed "all torrent actions," but the role deliberately withholds `torrents.delete_data`. That's exactly the kind of error that gets someone to grant a role they didn't mean to.

**`NAVIGATION.md` had seven dead links** (the whole Media Server Analytics group was missing its `/media-server-analytics/` prefix) and claimed Settings hosts an **API Keys UI that doesn't exist anywhere in the frontend**.

It also found `API.md` was missing four entire controllers and understated automation to 2 triggers / 7 actions when the source registers **14 and ~22**.

Waiting on the final agent (`IMDB_IMPORT.md`, `MEDIA_MANAGER.md`), then I'll commit the remainder, push, and deploy to both.

## MEDIA_MANAGER.md — 8 false claims fixed

**(a) FALSE → fixed**
1. **Post-download workflow order was wrong.** Doc had rename/move at step 3, metadata at step 4. Source runs **metadata *before* rename**, deliberately, so the new filename can use the fullest identity — `media-processing.service.ts:292-310` ("Stage 3 — metadata, fetched *before* the rename"; "Stage 4 — rename/move").
2. **Job-type enum listed a type that no longer exists.** Doc: `… | rename_preview | rename_execute | …`. Source `MediaJobType` = `library_scan | media_identification | metadata_fetch | artwork_fetch | subtitle_scan | rename_execute | library_organize | nfo_generate | media_server_refresh` — `media-processing-queue.service.ts:8-17`. (The schema comment at `schema.prisma:719` is also stale, but that's code, untouched.)
3. **Missing: restart reconciliation.** `MediaProcessingQueueService.onModuleInit` (`media-processing-queue.service.ts:43-70`) fails out orphaned `queued`/`running` rows with `"Interrupted by a service restart"`. Documented.
4. **"import schedule … there is no scheduled-jobs seed to enable" — FALSE.** `ImdbDatasetScheduler` (`imdb-dataset-scheduler.service.ts:32-66`) runs `imdb_dataset_auto_update` hourly, gated on `mode ∈ dataset|hybrid` + `autoDownloadEnabled`, at `autoUpdateIntervalHours` (default **168**, `imdb-settings.service.ts:98-100`). Added an "Automatic dataset refresh" section; `importSchedule` re-labelled legacy.
5. **"Seven `.tsv.gz` files are required" — FALSE.** Only `title.basics` is required (`imdb-dataset-importer.service.ts:53 hasMinimum`), the dataset path is optional (falls back to a managed dir, `imdb.service.ts:241-252`), and files are **downloaded** by `update-now` / auto-update (`imdb.service.ts:267-282`) — note `POST /dataset/import` downloads *nothing*.
6. **IMDb settings table missing 9 shipped keys** (`datasetBaseUrl`, `autoDownloadEnabled`, `autoUpdateIntervalHours`, `importStrategy`, `minImportYear`, `importTvShows`, `importAkas`, `importCrew`, `importPeople`) — `imdb-settings.service.ts:92-112`.
7. **Duplicate reason `file_hash` doesn't exist.** Detector emits only `external_id | show_season_episode | title_year | similar_filename` (`media-duplicate.service.ts:6-10`); `file_hash` survives only as a schema comment.
8. **Role grants understated.** Power User also holds `move_files` and **all five** `media_manager.imdb.*`; User holds `view` + `imdb.view` + `imdb.search` (`permissions.ts:218-232, 236-253`).

**Also added (shipped, undocumented):** `POST /libraries/:id/organize` (+ scan's organize pass and junk-cleanup rules), `GET /series`, `GET /series/episodes`, `POST /items/reidentify` in the REST table, `POST /items/:id/artwork/import`, `GET /artwork/:artworkId/image`, `GET|PATCH /settings/cleanup`, `POST /providers/tmdb/test`, IMDb `dataset/import/stop|update-now|reset`; the `/media/rename` page; `scanIntervalMinutes`'s scan-and-enrich scheduler (`media-library-scan-scheduler.service.ts:36`); scanner-time `parseItemIdentity` (`media-scanner.service.ts:135`).

**(b) Still true:** `move_files`/`delete`/`admin` unenforced (only in `manifests.ts:246-250`); 7 media triggers + 9 actions, 8 delegated (`automation.module.ts:59-108`); token table matches `buildTokens` exactly; library/mode/preset defaults match schema.

## MEDIA_SERVER_ANALYTICS.md — 4 fixed
1. **"*(Import lands in a later phase.)*" — FALSE**; Tautulli watch-history import ships (`analytics-import.service.ts`, controller lines 190-236). Rewritten to say what shipped vs. what remains.
2. **Session poll is 15s, not 30s** — `media-server-session.service.ts:51` (`@Interval('media_server_session_poll', 15_000)`). Two occurrences.
3. **"API (Phase 1)" table was ~2/3 short** — added 7 reports, `export/watch-history`, `meta/*`, `live/:id/artwork`, import-sources/import-jobs, newsletters, `settings/email`, `settings/newsletter-images`, and the unguarded `nl-image` route.
4. **Missing permission `media_server_analytics.export`** (`permissions.ts:103`); missing the hourly `media_server_metadata_sync` job (`media-server-sync.service.ts:35`) → documented. Roadmap: notifications ✅ and UI pages ✅ (both shipped); **automation triggers/actions genuinely still absent** — no `media_server.*` entry in `automation.module.ts:57-92`.

**(b) Still true:** snapshot persistence (no snapshot model); newsletter subscription management (`MediaServerNewsletter.recipientEmails` is a plain JSON list, `schema.prisma:941`).

## NOTIFICATION_CENTER.md — substantially accurate
Your lead is confirmed: providers are exactly **Email / Telegram / SMS(Twilio) / WhatsApp(Twilio)** (`provider-registry.ts:25-67`), and the doc already frames Discord/Slack correctly as *structurally supported, not shipped*. Seeded catalog is genuinely **47 rules** (`seed.service.ts:22-75`), groups match, workers/intervals match (`delivery.service.ts:123`, `provider-health.service.ts:24`), REST + RBAC match the controller.
**One fix:** the publisher list omitted **Media Acquisition → `media.missing_episode_filled`** (`missing-episode-search.service.ts:306-311`). Added.
**Still true:** the "remaining catalog events" list (`media_added`, `server_online/offline`, `torrent_added/failed/stalled`, `ratio_reached`, `rss.new_episode_available`, `media.duplicate`, `system.update_available`) — none are emitted anywhere.

## IMDB_IMPORT.md — 3 fixed

3. **Added "Search indexes"** — the runtime `CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY` pg_trgm builds (`imdb-trigram-index.service.ts:9-70`), which nothing documented.
**Still true:** strategy defaults, title filter, `IMDB_MIN_YEAR`=1970 / `IMDB_IMPORT_BATCH_SIZE`=5000 (`configuration.ts:105-108`), `title.principals` always skipped (`ALWAYS_SKIPPED_DATASETS`), all stat field names, ranking behaviour (`imdb-metadata.provider.ts:251-263`).

**Claude:** The build is running on synoplex (this is the first real exercise of the new docs stage). While it builds, here's the complete audit result.

**All 24 docs audited against source. 23 had stale or false claims; only `ARCHITECTURE.md` was clean** — which is the whole story: it's the one doc a Stop hook forces you to update on every architectural commit. Nothing enforced the other 23, so they froze at whatever phase each feature was in when someone last wrote about it.

The ones that would actually hurt someone:

- **`SECURITY.md` was silent on API keys** while the table, DTOs and `apikeys.manage` permission all exist — so a reader would reasonably assume the feature works. It now says plainly that a key **cannot authenticate a request**: `keyHash` is written and read nowhere. Also had `POWER_USER` wrong (it withholds `torrents.delete_data`, contrary to "all torrent actions") — exactly the error that gets someone to grant a role they didn't intend.
- **`DOCKER.md` documented a default login** (`admin`/`changeme123!`) that doesn't exist — `ADMIN_PASSWORD` is `:?required`.
- **`INDEXERS.md` described an auto-acquire bridge that no longer exists**: it said the winner goes to the evaluator and "the quality profile decides." It doesn't — the bridge selects via match preferences and calls `grabSelected()`, which skips the scorer and decision engine entirely.
- **`MEDIA_MANAGER.md` had the post-download workflow backwards** — metadata is fetched *before* the rename, deliberately, so the filename can use the fullest identity.

They also caught **my own bad fix**: I'd written that missing-episode scans are now scheduled. Only the *search* is; the catalogue↔library diff is still manual. Corrected.

Caveats that are genuinely still true were kept rather than scrubbed: `decide()` still never emits `replace_existing`, there are still no acquisition or media-server automation triggers, and missing movies are detected but never swept.

Pushed as `b2c1d77` + `11f1b35`. I'll report as soon as the build lands.

<result>I have the complete picture. Here are my findings.

## 1. Dashboard counters — the dash comes from the formatter, not a null check

**`/var/www/ultratorrent/apps/frontend/src/pages/DashboardPage.tsx:52-53`**
```ts
// Prefer live socket rates; fall back to the polled summary.
const downloadRate = stats?.downloadRate ?? summary?.downloadRate ?? 0;
const uploadRate = stats?.uploadRate ?? summary?.uploadRate ?? 0;
```

**`DashboardPage.tsx:68-81`** — the two counters:
```tsx
&lt;StatCard label={t('stats.download')} value={formatSpeed(downloadRate)} ... loading={summaryQuery.isLoading &amp;&amp; !stats} /&gt;
&lt;StatCard label={t('stats.upload')}   value={formatSpeed(uploadRate)}   ... loading={summaryQuery.isLoading &amp;&amp; !stats} /&gt;
```

There is no `?? '—'` and no null-guard in the JSX. Because of the `?? 0` chain, `downloadRate` is **always a number** — it can never be undefined. The dash is produced inside the formatter:

**`/var/www/ultratorrent/apps/frontend/src/lib/format.ts:20-24`**
```ts
/** Format bytes-per-second, e.g. "4.20 MB/s". Zero renders as an em dash. */
export function formatSpeed(bytesPerSecond: number | null | undefined): string {
  if (!bytesPerSecond || bytesPerSecond &lt;= 0) return '—';
  return `${formatBytes(bytesPerSecond)}/s`;
}
```

**So `'—'` is literally the rendering of `0`.** The counter isn't losing its data — it is being told the speed is zero. (Contrast `formatSpeedCompact`, `format.ts:27-30`, used by the top bar `AppShell.tsx:40`, which renders the same zero as `"0 B/s"` — which is why the header and the Dashboard disagree.)

Note also `summaryQuery.isLoading` is only true on the *first* fetch; React Query keeps `data` during the 15s background refetches, so the skeleton/`loading` path is not involved in the flicker.

## 2. Where the speeds come from — WebSocket, `stats:update`

**`/var/www/ultratorrent/apps/frontend/src/realtime/RealtimeContext.tsx:49-66`**
```ts
const offStats = wsClient.on(WS_EVENTS.STATS_UPDATE, (payload) =&gt; {
  setStats(payload.stats);                      // &lt;-- last writer wins, engineId ignored
  const now = Date.now();
  setBandwidth((prev) =&gt; {
    const sample: BandwidthSample = {
      t: now,
      label: new Date(now).toLocaleTimeString(...),
      down: payload.stats.downloadRate,
      up: payload.stats.uploadRate,
    };
    const next = [...prev, sample];
    return next.length &gt; MAX_SAMPLES ? next.slice(next.length - MAX_SAMPLES) : next;  // MAX_SAMPLES = 60
  });
});
```

Payload shape — **`/var/www/ultratorrent/packages/shared/src/events.ts:175-179`**: `{ engineId: string; stats: GlobalStats; at: string }`, and `GlobalStats` (**`packages/shared/src/torrent.ts:111-120`**) has `downloadRate` / `uploadRate` (plus limits, totals, counts). Event name `stats:update` (`events.ts:9`).

The polled `useQuery(['dashboard','summary'])` (`DashboardPage.tsx:37-41`, 15s) is only a fallback and, critically, `??` does **not** fall through a live `0`.

## 3. The graph — same source, same tick, but zero is a valid point

`BandwidthChart` (**`DashboardPage.tsx:172-179`**) reads `bandwidth` from the *same* `useRealtime()` context. Both the counters and the graph update on the exact same `stats:update` event — that's why they move in lockstep. The difference is that the graph plots `0` as a legitimate data point (and keeps its 60-sample history), while the counters run the *latest* sample through `formatSpeed`, which maps `0 → '—'`.

## 4. Backend emitter — one `stats:update` **per engine**, every 2s

**`/var/www/ultratorrent/apps/backend/src/modules/torrents/torrent-sync.service.ts:37-69`**
```ts
@Interval(2000)
async sync(): Promise&lt;void&gt; {
  if (this.syncing) return;
  this.syncing = true;
  try {
    for (const provider of this.registry.list()) {   // &lt;-- EVERY registered engine
      await this.syncEngine(provider);
    }
  } finally { this.syncing = false; }
}
...
this.realtime.broadcast(WS_EVENTS.STATS_UPDATE, { engineId: provider.engineId, stats, at });
```

The payload is never partial or `{}` — `getGlobalStats()` always returns a full `GlobalStats`, and on error the whole `syncEngine` try-block is skipped (only `engine:status` with `online:false` is emitted, `torrent-sync.service.ts:80-90`). But it **coerces missing values to 0**:

- **`qbittorrent.provider.ts:348-352`**: `downloadRate: transfer?.dl_info_speed ?? 0, uploadRate: transfer?.up_info_speed ?? 0`
- **`rtorrent.provider.ts:341-345`**: `downloadRate: num(down), uploadRate: num(up)`
- **`dashboard.module.ts:35-36`**: `downloadRate: stats?.downloadRate ?? 0`

So the wire never carries `null`/`undefined` speeds — it carries **`0`**, which is exactly what the UI renders as a dash.

## Best-supported hypothesis

**The counters are not losing data — they are receiving `0`, and `formatSpeed()` renders `0` as `'—'` (`format.ts:22`).**

The most likely source of that alternating `0`, and the one that explains the flicker being *in sync with the graph tick*, is a **last-writer-wins race across multiple engines** in `RealtimeContext.tsx:50`:

- `TorrentSyncService.sync()` loops over **all** registered providers every 2s and broadcasts **one `stats:update` per engine** (`torrent-sync.service.ts:42-44, 65-69`), each carrying that engine's *own* `engineId` and its *own* rates.
- The frontend does `setStats(payload.stats)` with **no `engineId` filter and no aggregation** — whichever engine broadcasts last overwrites the state.
- With 2+ engines configured where only one is actively transferring, `stats` alternates every tick: real rate → idle engine's `0` → real rate → `0`. The counters therefore flash a number and immediately flip to `'—'`, while the graph keeps drawing (it just appends `0` samples and sawtooths).

A quick way to confirm: if the throughput graph shows a **zigzag down to zero on every other sample**, and `bandwidth.samples` (`DashboardPage.tsx:107`) grows *twice* per 2s cycle, it's the multi-engine clobber. Corroborating evidence for that being a real bug and not a theory: `engineOnline` has the identical last-writer-wins flaw (`RealtimeContext.tsx:68-71`, `torrent-sync.service.ts:74-79, 84-89`) — a second, offline engine would also flip the Online/Offline badge on the same cadence.

If only **one** engine is configured, the same code still yields the flicker whenever that engine legitimately reports a momentary `0` (qBittorrent's `dl_info_speed` between pieces, checking/stalled states) — the display bug is `formatSpeed` conflating "zero" with "no data". Either way, `format.ts:22` is the line that turns a number into a dash, and the `??` chain at `DashboardPage.tsx:52-53` guarantees the summary fallback can never rescue a live `0`.</result>

**Claude:** Found it — and your deployment confirms it.

## The cause: two engines, and the dashboard lets them overwrite each other

`synoplex` has **two engines enabled**:

```
 qBittorrent | qbittorrent | t
 rTorrent    | rtorrent    | t
```

The backend's sync loop runs every 2 seconds and broadcasts **one `stats:update` per engine** — each carrying only *that* engine's rates (`torrent-sync.service.ts:42-44`):

```ts
@Interval(2000)
async sync() {
  for (const provider of this.registry.list()) {   // every engine
    await this.syncEngine(provider);               // → broadcast stats:update
  }
}
```

But the frontend just takes whichever arrives last, ignoring which engine it came from (`RealtimeContext.tsx:50`):

```ts
const offStats = wsClient.on(WS_EVENTS.STATS_UPDATE, (payload) => {
  setStats(payload.stats);   // no engineId filter, no aggregation — last writer wins
```

So on every tick the state gets clobbered twice: qBittorrent reports its real rate, then rTorrent (idle) immediately overwrites it with **0**. Number → zero → number → zero, forever.

## Why zero renders as a dash

The counter never goes "empty" — it's being told the speed is genuinely `0`, and the formatter turns that into an em dash (`format.ts:20-23`):

```ts
export function formatSpeed(bytesPerSecond: number | null | undefined): string {
  if (!bytesPerSecond || bytesPerSecond <= 0) return '—';   // 0 → '—'
  return `${formatBytes(bytesPerSecond)}/s`;
}
```

That's also why it's **exactly in sync with the graph**: both read the same event. The graph just plots `0` as a legitimate point, so instead of flickering it sawtooths down to zero on alternating samples. (Worth checking — if your graph zigzags to the floor every other sample, that's the same bug drawing itself.)

Two corroborating details: the top bar uses `formatSpeedCompact`, which renders the same zero as `"0 B/s"` — which is why the header and dashboard disagree. And `engineOnline` has the identical last-writer-wins flaw (`RealtimeContext.tsx:68`), so if one engine ever goes offline your Online/Offline badge will flip on the same 2-second cadence.

## The fix

Keep the per-engine stats in a map keyed by `engineId` and **sum** them, rather than letting the last event win — a dashboard headline figure should be total throughput across all engines anyway. Same treatment for `engineOnline` (online if *any* engine is up, with the offline one surfaced separately).

Want me to implement that? It's contained to `RealtimeContext.tsx`, and I'd keep `formatSpeed`'s dash-for-zero as-is, since a true idle really should read as "—".

## What was wrong

Your `synoplex` box has **two engines enabled** (qBittorrent + rTorrent). The backend syncs both on one 2-second tick and broadcasts a *separate* `stats:update` per engine. `RealtimeContext` kept only the newest event and ignored its `engineId` — so every tick published qBittorrent's real rate, then instantly overwrote it with rTorrent's idle **0**. And `formatSpeed(0)` renders as `—`.

So the counter was never "losing" its data. It was being told the speed was zero, twice a second. Same reason the graph moved in lockstep: it plots the identical values, but zero is a legal point, so instead of flickering it sawtoothed to the floor on alternating samples.

## What I changed

All in `RealtimeContext.tsx` — the last report is now kept **per engine** and the totals derived from all of them:

- **Counters** show combined throughput across engines instead of whichever engine spoke last.
- **The chart** folds one sync round into a single point, rather than plotting one point per engine (a two-engine setup was drawing twice as many points as it should).
- **The online badge** reports up if *any* engine is up. This was the same bug wearing a different hat — one offline engine would have flipped your Online/Offline badge every 2 seconds.
- **Rate limits** are combined, not summed: `0` means unlimited, so one unbounded engine leaves the aggregate unbounded instead of inventing a ceiling that doesn't exist.

I left `formatSpeed`'s dash-for-zero alone — a genuinely idle client *should* read as `—`.

## Verification

Five new tests in `RealtimeContext.test.tsx`. The important part: **three of them fail against the old code** and pass against the new, and they fail on exactly the three symptoms you'd see — the counter clobber, the doubled chart points, and the badge flip. Full frontend suite is green (13 files, 70 tests) and `tsc --noEmit` is clean.

## 1. The :8080 redirect — reproduced and fixed

```
$ curl -sI http://localhost:8888/docs
HTTP/1.1 301 Moved Permanently
Location: http://localhost:8080/docs/     ← dead port
```

The frontend container listens on **8080** but is published on **8888**. nginx composes absolute redirects from the port it *listens* on, so the first visit to `/docs` sent you to a port that isn't open. The config did have `absolute_redirect off` — but only inside `location /docs/`, and the redirect fires from `location = /docs`. It's now set at **server level**, since directory redirects are generated outside any location block.

## 2. The screenshots aren't broken — they don't exist

All 126 images load fine (200, ~12 KB each). They're the **placeholder slates** I generated, because no real screenshots have ever been captured. So you're looking at empty gray panels, which reads as "missing" — and fairly so. I can't capture them here (no browser on this box), but each one names the exact screen in an admonition above it, and dropping in a real capture is just overwriting the file.

## 3. The errors — two kinds, both real

**Web-server errors:** `/docs/modules` worked but `/docs/modules/` **404'd**. Docusaurus emits both `modules.html` *and* a `modules/` directory of leaf pages, so a trailing slash resolved to the directory, which has no `index.html`. Now stripped before `try_files`. Separately, `/docs/learn` and `/docs/help` 404'd because those sections **had no landing page at all** — their `_category_.json` declared no link. Both now have one.

**Code errors:** this is the one you couldn't have known the size of. I parsed all 120 Mermaid diagrams with Mermaid itself, and **8 were failing** — rendering as red "Syntax error in text" boxes. Four distinct causes:

- a `;` inside a sequence-diagram message silently ends the statement
- `API[/api/torrents]` is Mermaid's *parallelogram shape*, not a label
- a dotted-link label containing dots (`-.torrent.completed.->`) needs the pipe form
- `graph LR "auth" --> "rbac"` — a quoted bare node id is invalid

That last one came out of `generate-reference.mjs`, so I fixed the **generator**, not the output — otherwise it would regenerate broken on every build. All 120 now parse clean, and the strict build is green (both locales).

The host site at **https://ultratorrent.ultranetpr.net/docs/** is rebuilt and every route verified. The **bundled** docs in the container still carry the old build — the nginx and diagram fixes only reach synoplex/ehr-qnap on an image rebuild. Want me to roll that out?
